opp.name: Newrez [S2P] Feb'26
opp.next_step: Joyce mentioned about having an internal discussion with her manager about Zycus, since they do not have any tools in place for automation right now
opp.ais_why:
• On the 2/11/26 call, customer Joyce Kornfeld voiced 'interest in AI applications for indirect services within financial services' and committed to reviewing the Hackett Group Pinnacle webinar on AI trends — clear prospect-side AI pull.
• Same-day demo covered agentic AI flows for invoice capture, validation, matching and routing; piloting autonomous negotiation agents on tail spend; Merlin Intake on MS Teams; and the customer pressed into fraud-detection AI specifics.
• Rep Edward Dlugosz has consistently anchored the pitch on AI — even sending the Hackett Pinnacle AI webinar. Internal Zycus discussion is underway per SF, last activity 4/14/26.

SF Next_Step__c: Joyce mentioned internal discussion with her manager about Zycus 'since they do not have any tools in place for automation right now'
metrics.narrative: Joyce articulated operational pain tied to manual, offshore-dependent processes and disparate legacy systems across Newrez's recently acquired subsidiaries, but did not quantify cost, headcount, cycle time savings, or error-rate impact on the 11 Feb call. She stated 'all the repetitive tasks get sent offshore, but they're manual today' and confirmed challenges with 'visibility' and 'data gathering' across subsidiaries lacking integrated procurement systems. When Ed presented Zycus's automated invoice processing, supplier onboarding, and tail-spend negotiation agents, Joyce responded positively ('That is always a challenge for sure,' 'It's fascinating,' 'Very interesting') but did not commit to a specific savings target or timeline. The pain is confirmed and felt (Joyce was literally in a meeting on intake process challenges immediately before the Zycus call), but the business case remains qualitative. Quantifying the offshore labor cost, manual AP workload, and maverick-spend leakage is the immediate next step to build urgency and justify budget.

sources: Avoma call 11 Feb 2026, Joyce: 'I will definitely share this with my manager and my peer managers'
Avoma call 11 Feb 2026, Joyce: 'we talk about options and strategies because we are dealing with some legacy applications that aren't as efficient'
Avoma call 11 Feb 2026, Joyce positive engagement: 'That is always a challenge,' 'It's fascinating,' 'Very interesting'
SF Next_Step__c (25 Jun): 'Joyce mentioned about having an internal discussion with her manager about Zycus'
135-day gap since 11 Feb call with no intro to manager or escalation
champion.narrative: Joyce Kornfeld (Senior Sourcing and Procurement Professional) is a developing champion. On the 11 Feb call she confirmed genuine pain, engaged enthusiastically with the relevant stakeholder/automation story (especially Merlin Intake and agentic workflows), and explicitly committed to share the Zycus presentation with her manager and peer managers. She stated 'I will definitely share this with my manager and my peer managers… we talk about options and strategies because we are dealing with some legacy applications that aren't as efficient.' This is champion-building in progress—Joyce is advocating internally and positioning Zycus as a solution to Newrez's intake, adoption, and integration challenges. However, her strength is LIMITED by three factors: (1) she is an individual contributor with no budget authority ('I can't promise anything beyond' sharing the info), (2) she has NOT facilitated an introduction to her manager or peer managers in the 135 days since the call, and (3) she stated 'we're not actively looking right now,' meaning she is not driving an urgent evaluation. The Next_Step__c (25 Jun) references her intent to have 'an internal discussion with her manager,' but that discussion has not been confirmed or followed up. Joyce is engaged and positive but PASSIVE—she is waiting for internal alignment rather than actively pulling Zycus into the organization. The next move is to re-engage Joyce, confirm the internal discussion outcome, and convert her advocacy into a SCHEDULED meeting with her manager to elevate the conversation and map the Economic Buyer.

sources: Avoma call 11 Feb 2026, Joyce: 'we have one primary vendor' (unnamed)
SF Competitors__c: unknown
No competitor mentioned in Next_Step__c, call transcripts, or tasks
competition.narrative: No competitors named or identified across any source—Avoma calls, Salesforce Competitors__c (unknown), Next_Step__c, or other synthesis-map fields. Joyce stated Newrez has 'one primary vendor' for procurement but did not name it on the 11 Feb call. She also mentioned past P2P rollout failures and confirmed 'we're not actively looking right now,' suggesting this is early discovery rather than an active competitive bake-off. The unnamed incumbent is a potential threat IF Newrez's 'one primary vendor' has AI/automation roadmap credibility, but Joyce's enthusiasm for Zycus's Merlin Intake ('We tried to do something like this before AI technology existed') and agentic workflows ('That is always a challenge') suggests the incumbent is NOT solving her intake and adoption pain. The competitive landscape is UNKNOWN, not clear. Possible scenarios: (1) Newrez runs an RFP and Zycus faces SAP Ariba, Coupa, or Ivalua; (2) the incumbent (possibly Jaggaer, GEP, or another mid-market S2P suite) proposes an upgrade/module add; (3) Newrez sole-sources Zycus based on Joyce's advocacy and manager approval. The next step is to ask Joyce (or her manager) directly: Who is your current vendor? Are you considering other solutions alongside Zycus? What would trigger a formal RFP?

identify_pain.narrative: Joyce owns and articulated three operational pains on the 11 Feb call, all confirmed as current and felt: (1) Manual, offshore-dependent AP and procurement processes—'all the repetitive tasks get sent offshore, but they're manual today'—creating cost, cycle-time drag, and error risk. (2) Disparate legacy systems across Newrez's acquired subsidiaries—'we have a lot of companies without any systems' and 'visibility is a challenge'—preventing consolidated spend analysis, contract compliance tracking, and cross-entity leverage. (3) Poor user adoption and intake confusion—Joyce was literally in a meeting on 'how do I raise a request' intake process challenges immediately before the Zycus call, and she confirmed past P2P rollout failures due to 'low user adoption and complicated processes.' When Ed demonstrated Zycus's agentic AI (automated invoice matching, supplier onboarding, tail-spend negotiation) and Merlin Intake's MS Teams conversational UI, Joyce responded with enthusiasm ('That is always a challenge for sure,' 'It's fascinating,' 'Very interesting,' 'We tried to do something like this before AI technology existed'). The pain is real, owned by Joyce and her procurement/AP team, and tied to Newrez's post-acquisition integration complexity. The missing piece is QUANTIFICATION—offshore labor cost, manual AP headcount, maverick-spend leakage, and cycle-time benchmarks—needed to build urgency and justify budget.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement policy, and InfoSec requirements are completely unknown. Joyce did not mention MSA/SOW, redlining, legal approval gates, InfoSec/security review, or procurement channel on either call. As a mortgage financial services company, Newrez almost certainly has InfoSec diligence (data privacy, SOC 2, third-party risk assessment) and legal contracting requirements, but none have been surfaced. There is no mention of whether Newrez uses a preferred vendor list, a three-bid procurement policy, or a centralized contracting function. The account contact list includes a 'Director Procurement' (William Shanker) and several Strategic Sourcing managers, suggesting a formal procurement function exists, but their involvement in THIS deal is unconfirmed. The next step is to ask Joyce (or her manager once engaged) about Newrez's vendor onboarding and contracting process: Does a new S2P platform require legal review? Who runs InfoSec diligence? Is there a preferred procurement approval workflow? Without this visibility, timeline and close-date planning is guesswork.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified. Joyce Kornfeld is a Senior Sourcing and Procurement Professional (individual contributor, not budget authority) and explicitly stated on 11 Feb that 'we're not actively looking right now' and 'I can't promise anything beyond' sharing the information with her manager and peer managers. She confirmed she has a manager (unnamed) and peer managers who would evaluate options, but none have been named, introduced, or engaged. The account-level contact list includes three CFO-titled contacts (Felicia Grumet, Egan Dan, Cathy Dondzila), but none have appeared on calls, been referenced in Next_Step__c, or been confirmed as involved in THIS opportunity's scope. Budget authority for a $110k S2P suite + Merlin AI platform almost certainly sits at Director/VP Procurement or CFO level, not with the relevant stakeholder evidence shows Joyce is a champion-in-development (she committed to share internally), but the Economic Buyer is unmapped. The next step is for Joyce or the deal owner to facilitate an introduction to her manager (likely Director or VP Strategic Sourcing) and to the finance leader who controls procurement/AP technology budget.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is unmapped. Joyce stated 'we're not actively looking right now' and 'I can't promise anything beyond' sharing the Zycus presentation with her manager and peer managers, indicating this is a DISCOVERY/education stage, not an active procurement. She did not name an evaluation timeline, a committee, an RFP schedule, approval gates, or a forcing event. The call sequence shows only Joyce (individual contributor) on two intro calls (11 Feb 2026), with no escalation, no additional stakeholders, and no follow-up engagement in the subsequent 135 days. The Next_Step__c (25 Jun) references Joyce's intent to have 'an internal discussion with her manager,' but that discussion has not been confirmed or scheduled. The process likely requires: (1) Joyce's manager and peer managers to review and validate pain/fit, (2) a business case with quantified ROI to justify budget, (3) CFO or VP Finance approval for a $110k+ S2P platform, and (4) possibly an RFP or vendor comparison if procurement policy mandates competitive bidding. None of these steps have been initiated or dated. The immediate move is to re-engage Joyce, confirm whether the internal discussion occurred, and request an introduction to her manager to map the approval chain.

decision_criteria.narrative: Joyce surfaced three clear buying criteria on the 11 Feb call, all tied to past rollout failures: (1) user adoption—she referenced Spend Matters' stat that '90% of procurement software rollouts fail… due to low user adoption and complicated processes' and confirmed 'Been there. Rolled out a few P2P applications in my past, so completely agree.' (2) Ease of use / intuitive intake—she was in a meeting on intake process challenges immediately before the Zycus call and responded enthusiastically to Merlin Intake's MS Teams conversational UI: 'We tried to do something like this before AI technology existed… it's interesting that the automation technology exists nowadays.' (3) System integration and configurability—she asked 'So it is configurable that way?' when Ed described unified field propagation across modules, and confirmed Newrez runs 'one primary vendor' but has 'a lot of companies without any systems' post-acquisition, meaning integration/scalability across disparate entities is critical. These are USE-CASE criteria, not formal RFP weightings. The formal decision process (RFI, RFP, scoring model, committee composition) is unknown—Joyce has not mentioned an active evaluation, a shortlist, or a timeline. The next step is to confirm whether Newrez will run a formal RFP or a use-case-driven selection, who owns the requirements document, and what technical/commercial gates exist.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Joyce is AI Curious and aligned with Zycus's AI/automation roadmap. She explicitly stated interest in 'what's out there in the market' with 'new introductions with AI capabilities' and confirmed she wants to 'make our jobs as easy as possible.' She engaged enthusiastically with Merlin Intake, agentic workflows, and AI-driven spend analysis, and committed to review the Hackett Group Pinnacle AI webinar. Her past P2P adoption failures and current intake process challenges make her a strong fit for AI-driven user adoption solutions. AIS fields are null, so this read is based entirely on call content.

She is positive and committed but PASSIVE—waiting for internal alignment rather than actively driving the deal. Champion strength is WEAK due to lack of follow-through and executive access.

items.action: Build quantified ROI model for Newrez: offshore labor cost savings, AP automation headcount reduction, maverick-spend recovery.

items.expected_effect: Transforms qualitative pain into a quantified business case that justifies $110k investment and creates urgency. Joyce's manager (likely Director/VP Strategic Sourcing) can validate the business case, confirm the decision process and timeline, and facilitate introduction to the CFO or the relevant stakeholder (Economic Buyer) who controls procurement/AP technology budget. items.action: Ask Joyce (or her manager once engaged) to name the incumbent vendor and confirm competitive landscape.

If the incumbent is a legacy S2P suite (Jaggaer, Basware, older Ariba) with no AI roadmap, Zycus's Merlin/agentic AI story is a strong wedge. If the incumbent is Coupa, SAP Ariba, or Ivalua with AI investment, Zycus must prove better adoption (Merlin Intake) and organic AI integration (one codebase). Knowing the competitor allows precise differentiation.

items.expected_effect: Demonstrates Zycus's differentiation (MS Teams conversational UI, agentic invoice/supplier workflows) in Newrez's real-world context, reinforces Joyce's advocacy, and provides her manager with concrete proof of fit. A live demo of Merlin Intake guiding a user through NDA request or supplier onboarding, plus agentic AP automation, will validate that Zycus solves Newrez's adoption and automation pain better than the unnamed incumbent. Schedule this AFTER Joyce's manager is engaged so the demo includes the decision-maker.

confidence_signals.summary: Mixed. Positive: Joyce confirmed genuine pain, engaged enthusiastically with Zycus's AI story, and committed to share with her manager and peer managers. Negative: 135-day buyer silence since 11 Feb, no manager introduction, no escalation, no multi-threading, no budget authority, and Joyce stated 'we're not actively looking right now.' The deal is cooling—Joyce is positive but passive, and without re-engagement within 7 days the champion will fade.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 17 Feb 2026, stage Qualified, 129 days in stage (17 Feb to 26 Jun). Last buyer touchpoint 11 Feb 2026 (135 days ago). Last Salesforce activity 25 Jun 2026 (1 day ago, rep-side). Close date 06 Feb 2027 is 225 days away. To move from Qualified through Formal Evaluation, Shortlisted, Vendor Selected, Negotiation, and Contract In Progress to close in 7.5 months requires buyer re-engagement within 7 days, demo/workshop within 30 days, Economic Buyer mapping within 45 days, and commercials by Q4 2026. Current single-thread (Joyce, individual contributor) and zero executive contact after 135 days of buyer silence means the timeline is ambitious but the deal is fundamentally healthy—Joyce expressed clear pain (manual processes, disparate systems, low user adoption) and explicit interest in sharing the solution with her manager and peer managers on the 11 Feb call. The ONLY issue is timing and multi-threading; the engagement is real.
evidence: Last buyer call 11 Feb 2026 (135 days ago): Joyce confirmed pain (manual offshore processes, legacy system inefficiencies, intake challenges) and committed to share Zycus presentation with her manager and peer managers.
Next_Step__c (current, dated 25 Jun per LastActivityDate): Joyce mentioned internal discussion with her manager about Zycus since they lack automation tools.
Zero buyer calls or verified buyer engagement since 11 Feb; rep-side activity 25 Jun indicates deal team is tracking but buyer has not re-engaged.

Stage Qualified for 129 days with no progression to Formal Evaluation signals slow buyer process, not disinterest—Joyce stated 'we're not actively looking right now' but expressed intent to explore options for legacy inefficiencies.

Close date 06 Feb 2027 (225 days) is realistic IF Joyce's manager and peer managers engage within 30 days and IF Economic Buyer/CFO-level sponsorship emerges by Q3 2026, but current single-thread and 135-day silence make the date optimistic without immediate re-engagement.
north_star_verdict.headline: 135 days of buyer silence since Joyce's 11-Feb call erases momentum. Pain and intent were genuine, but zero executive engagement or manager follow-through signals stalled internal process. Feb-2027 close unrealistic without immediate re-engagement and multi-threading.

best_practice_check.summary: Three immediate win levers: (1) Re-engage Joyce within 7 days to confirm internal discussion outcome and convert her passive advocacy into a scheduled meeting with her manager—135 days of silence after a positive discovery call is the single highest risk. (2) Build a quantified business case (offshore labor cost, manual AP headcount, maverick-spend leakage) to justify budget and create urgency—Joyce's pain is real but qualitative; an ROI model tied to Newrez's post-acquisition integration complexity will move the conversation from 'interesting' to 'necessary.' (3) Multi-thread beyond Joyce to her manager (likely Director/VP Strategic Sourcing) and map the Economic Buyer (CFO or VP Finance)—a single individual-contributor contact 225 days from close with no executive visibility is a ghost risk, not a pipeline deal.

competitive_position.summary: No competitors identified. Joyce stated Newrez has 'one primary vendor' (unnamed) for procurement on 11 Feb call but did not name it or mention other vendors being evaluated. The unnamed incumbent is a potential threat if it has AI/automation credibility, but Joyce's enthusiasm for Zycus's Merlin Intake and agentic workflows suggests the incumbent is not solving her intake and adoption pain. Competitive landscape is UNKNOWN—possible incumbent upgrade, possible RFP with Ariba/Coupa/Ivalua, or possible sole-source based on Joyce's advocacy. With zero active competitors named and Joyce's positive engagement, Zycus is currently UNCONTESTED, but this is a visibility gap, not a confirmed win. The immediate move is to ask Joyce (or her manager) directly: Who is your current vendor, are you considering other solutions, and what would trigger a formal RFP?

ai_positioning_strength.score: Strong at champion level, unconfirmed at Economic Buyer level
ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus's AI/automation story is the CORE value proposition and resonated strongly with Joyce on 11 Feb. She was in a meeting on intake process challenges immediately before the Zycus call, and Merlin Intake's MS Teams conversational UI directly addressed her pain ('We tried to do something like this before AI technology existed'). Agentic AI workflows (invoice automation, supplier onboarding, tail-spend negotiation) solved her manual offshore process pain ('That is always a challenge for sure. It's fascinating.'). Ed positioned AI as the differentiator vs. competitors' 'bolted-together' architectures, and Joyce confirmed Zycus's evolution ('I think I had looked at Zycus a long time ago. This is very interesting to see how it's evolved.'). AI positioning is STRONG at the champion level, but unconfirmed with Economic Buyer or decision committee. The risk is that Joyce's manager or finance leader prioritizes cost over innovation, or the unnamed incumbent proposes a 'good enough' AI roadmap. The next move is to position Zycus's Merlin/agentic AI story with Joyce's manager and tie it to quantified ROI (AP automation headcount savings, tail-spend recovery) to prove AI is not a 'nice to have' but a business-case driver.

items.quote: We tried to do something like this before AI technology existed on our intranet… it's interesting that the automation technology exists nowadays to guide people automatically.
All the repetitive tasks get sent offshore, but they're manual today. That is always a challenge for sure. It's fascinating.

So it is configurable that way? [Ed: Yes, highly configurable, one code base, field in CLM auto-available in all modules.] I think I had looked at Zycus a long time ago. This is very interesting to see how it's evolved.
items.criterion: System integration and configurability across disparate acquired entities

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Zycus is strongly aligned with Joyce's three stated pain points and buying criteria: (1) Merlin Intake's MS Teams conversational UI directly addresses her intake process challenges and past P2P adoption failures; (2) agentic AI workflows (invoice automation, supplier onboarding, tail-spend negotiation) solve her manual offshore process pain; (3) unified codebase and cross-module configurability address her disparate-systems and post-acquisition integration challenges. Joyce responded enthusiastically on 11 Feb ('That is always a challenge,' 'It's fascinating,' 'We tried to do something like this before AI technology existed'), confirming product-market fit. The gap is QUANTIFICATION—Joyce has not tied Zycus's capabilities to specific cost savings, headcount reduction, or cycle-time improvement targets, and she has not validated fit with her manager, peer managers, or Economic Buyer. Fit is strong at the user/champion level but unconfirmed at decision-maker level.

hard.ais_why:
• On the 2/11/26 call, customer Joyce Kornfeld voiced 'interest in AI applications for indirect services within financial services' and committed to reviewing the Hackett Group Pinnacle webinar on AI trends — clear prospect-side AI pull.
• Same-day demo covered agentic AI flows for invoice capture, validation, matching and routing; piloting autonomous negotiation agents on tail spend; Merlin Intake on MS Teams; and the customer pressed into fraud-detection AI specifics.
• Rep Edward Dlugosz has consistently anchored the pitch on AI — even sending the Hackett Pinnacle AI webinar. Internal Zycus discussion is underway per SF, last activity 4/14/26.
hard.next_step: Joyce mentioned about having an internal discussion with her manager about Zycus, since they do not have any tools in place for automation right now
